title: The Wreck
content:
Hide me, Mother! my Fathers belongï¿½d to the church of old,
I am driven by storm and sin and death to the ancient fold,
I cling to the Catholic Cross once more, to the Faith that saves,
My brain is full of the crash of wrecks, and the roar of waves,
My life itself is a wreck, I have sullied a noble name,
I am flung from the rushing tide of the world as a waif of shame,
I am roused by the wail of a child, and awake to a livid light,
And a ghastlier face than ever has haunted a grave by night,
I would hide from the storm without, I would flee from the storm within,
I would make my life one prayer for a soul that died in his sin,
I was the tempter, Mother, and mine was the deeper fall;
I will sit at your feet, I will hide my face, I will tell you all.

II.
He that they gave me to, Mother, a heedless and innocent brideï¿½
I never have wrongï¿½d his heart, I have only wounded his prideï¿½
Spain in his blood and the Jewï¿½ï¿½dark visaged, stately and tallï¿½
A princelier looking man never stept throï¿½ a Princeï¿½s hall.
And who, when his anger was kindled, would venture to give him the nay?
And a man men fear is a man to be loved by the women they say.
And I could have loved him too, if the blossom can doat on the blight,
Or the young green leaf rejoice in the frost that sears it at night;
He would open the books that I prized, and toss them away with a yawn,
Repellï¿½d by the magnet of Art to the which my nature was drawn,
The word of the Poet by whom the deeps of the world are stirrï¿½d,
The music that robes it in language beneath and beyond the word!
My Shelley would fall from my hands when he cast a contemptuous glance
From where he was poring over his Tables of Trade and Finance;
My hands, when I heard him coming would drop from the chords or the keys,
But ever I failï¿½d to please him, however I strove to pleaseï¿½
All day long far-off in the cloud of the city, and there
Lost, head and heart, in the chances of dividend, consol, and shareï¿½
And at home if I sought for a kindly caress, being woman and weak,
His formal kiss fell chill as a flake of snow on the cheek:
And so, when I bore him a girl, when I held it aloft in my joy,
He lookï¿½d at it coldly, and said to me ï¿½Pity it isnï¿½t a boy.ï¿½
The one thing given me, to love and to live for, glanced at in scorn!
The child that I felt I could die forï¿½as if she were basely born!
I had lived a wild-flower life, I was planted now in a tomb;
The daisy will shut to the shadow, I closed my heart to the gloom;
I threw myself all abroadï¿½I would play my part with the young
By the low foot-lights of the worldï¿½and I caught the wreath that was flung.

III.
Mother, I have notï¿½however their tongues may have babbled of meï¿½
Sinnï¿½d throï¿½ an animal vileness, for all but a dwarf was he,
And all but a hunchback too; and I lookï¿½d at him, first, askance,
With pityï¿½not he the knight for an amorous girlï¿½s romance!
Thoï¿½ wealthy enough to have baskï¿½d in the light of a dowerless smile,
Having lands at home and abroad in a rich West-Indian isle;
But I came on him once at a ball, the heart of a listening crowdï¿½
Why, what a brow was there! he was seatedï¿½speaking aloud
To women, the flower of the time, and men at the helm of stateï¿½
Flowing with easy greatness and touching on all things great,
Science, philosophy, songï¿½till I felt myself ready to weep
For I knew not what, when I heard that voice,ï¿½as mellow and deep
As a psalm by a mighty master and pealï¿½d from an organ,ï¿½roll
Rising and fallingï¿½for, Mother, the voice was the voice of the soul;
And the sun of the soul made day in the dark of his wonderful eyes.
Here was the hand that would help me, would heal meï¿½the heart that was wise!
And he, poor man, when he learnt that I hated the ring I wore,
He helpt me with death, and he healï¿½d me with sorrow for evermore.

IV.
For I broke the bond. That day my nurse had brought me the child.
The small sweet face was flushï¿½d, but it cooï¿½d to the Mother and smiled.
ï¿½Anything ailing,ï¿½ I askï¿½d her, ï¿½with baby?ï¿½ She shook her head,
And the Motherless Mother kissï¿½d it, and turnï¿½d in her haste and fled.

V.
Low warm winds had gently breathed us away from the landï¿½
Ten long sweet summer days upon deck, sitting hand in handï¿½
When he clothed a naked mind with the wisdom and wealth of his own,
And I bowï¿½d myself down as a slave to his intellectual throne,
When he coinï¿½d into English gold some treasure of classical song,
When he flouted a statesmanï¿½s error, or flamed at a public wrong,
When he rose as it were on the wings of an eagle beyond me, and past
Over the range and the change of the world from the first to the last,
When lie spoke of his tropical home in the canes by the purple tide,
And the high star-crowns of his palms on the deep-wooded mountain-side,
And cliffs all robed in lianas that dropt to the brink of his bay,
And trees like the towers of a minster, the sons of a winterless day.
ï¿½Paradise there!ï¿½ so he said, but I seemï¿½d in Paradise then
With the first great love I had felt for the first and greatest of men;
Ten long days of summer and sinï¿½if it must be soï¿½
But days of a larger light than I ever again shall knowï¿½
Days that will glimmer, I fear, throï¿½ life to my latest breath;
ï¿½No frost there,ï¿½ so he said, ï¿½as in truest Love no Death.ï¿½

VI.
Mother, one morning a bird with a warble plaintively sweet
Perchï¿½d on the shrouds, and then fell fluttering down at my feet;
I took it, he made it a cage, we fondled it, Stephen and I,
But it died, and I thought of the child for a moment, I scarce know why.

VII.
But if sin be sin, not inherited fate, as many will say,
My sin to my desolate little one found me at sea on a day,
When her orphan wail came borne in the shriek of a growing wind,
And a voice rang out in the thunders of Ocean and Heaven ï¿½Thou hast sinnï¿½d.ï¿½
And down in the cabin were we, for the towering crest of the tides
Plunged on the vessel and swept in a cataract off from her sides,
And ever the great storm grew with a howl and a hoot of the blast
In the rigging, voices of hellï¿½then came the crash of the mast.
ï¿½The wages of sin is death,ï¿½ and there I began to weep,
ï¿½I am the Jonah, the crew should cast me into the deep,
For ah God, what a heart was mine to forsake her even for you.ï¿½
ï¿½Never the heart among women,ï¿½ he said, ï¿½more tender and true.ï¿½
ï¿½The heart! not a motherï¿½s heart, when I left my darling alone.ï¿½
ï¿½Comfort yourself, for the heart of the father will care for his own.ï¿½
ï¿½The heart of the father will spurn her,ï¿½ I cried, ï¿½for the sin of the wife,
The cloud of the motherï¿½s shame will enfold her and darken her life.ï¿½
Then his pale face twitchï¿½d; ï¿½O Stephen, I love you, I love you, and yetï¿½ï¿½
As I leanï¿½d away from his armsï¿½ï¿½would God, we had never met!ï¿½
And he spoke notï¿½only the storm; till after a little, I yearnï¿½d
For his voice again, and he callï¿½d to me ï¿½Kiss me!ï¿½ and thereï¿½as I turnï¿½dï¿½
ï¿½The heart, the heart!ï¿½ I kissï¿½d him, I clung to the sinking form,
And the storm went roaring above us, and heï¿½was out of the storm.

VIII.
And then, then, Mother, the ship staggerï¿½d under a thunderous shock,
That shook us asunder, as if she had struck and crashï¿½d on a rock;
For a huge sea smote every soul from the decks of The Falcon but one;
All of them, all but the man that was lashï¿½d to the helm had gone;
And I fellï¿½and the storm and the days went by, but I knew no moreï¿½
Lost myselfï¿½lay like the dead by the dead on the cabin floor,
Dead to the death beside me, and lost to the loss that was mine,
With a dim dream, now and then, of a hand giving bread and wine,
Till I woke from the trance, and the ship stood still, and the skies were blue,
But the face I had known, O Mother, was not the face that I knew.

IX.
The strange misfeaturing mask that I saw so amazed me, that I
Stumbled on deck, half mad. I would fling myself over and die!
But oneï¿½he was waving a flagï¿½the one man left on the wreckï¿½
ï¿½Womanï¿½ï¿½he graspt at my armï¿½ï¿½stay thereï¿½ï¿½I crouchï¿½d upon deckï¿½
ï¿½We are sinking, and yet thereï¿½s hope look yonder,ï¿½ he cried, ï¿½a sailï¿½
In a tone so rough that I broke into passionate tears, and the wail
Of a beaten babe, till I saw that a boat was nearing usï¿½then
All on a sudden I thought, I shall look on the child again.

X.
They lowerï¿½d me down the side, and there in the boat I lay
With sad eyes fixt on the lost sea-home, as we glided away,
And I sighï¿½d, as the low dark hull dipt under the smiling main,
ï¿½Had I stayï¿½d with him, I had nowï¿½with himï¿½been out of my pain.ï¿½

XI.
They took us aboard: the crew were gentle, the captain kind;
But I was the lonely slave of an often-wandering mind;
For whenever a rougher gust might tumble a stormier wave,
ï¿½O Stephen,ï¿½ I moanï¿½d, ï¿½I am coming to thee in thine Ocean-grave.ï¿½
And again, when a balmier breeze curlï¿½d over a peacefuller sea,
I found myself moaning again ï¿½O child, I am coming to thee.ï¿½

XII.
The broad white brow of the Isleï¿½that bay with the colourï¿½d sandï¿½
Rich was the rose of sunset there, as we drew to the land;
All so quiet the ripple would hardly blanch into spray
At the feet of the cliff; and I prayï¿½dï¿½ï¿½my childï¿½ï¿½for I still could prayï¿½
ï¿½May her life be as blissfully calm, be never gloomï¿½d by the curse
Of a sin, not hers!ï¿½
Was it well with the child?
I wrote to the nurse
Who had borne my flower on her hireling heart; and an answer came
Not from the nurseï¿½nor yet to the wifeï¿½to her maiden name!
I shook as I openï¿½d the letterï¿½I knew that hand too wellï¿½
And from it a scrap, clipt out of the ï¿½deathsï¿½ in a paper, fell.
ï¿½Ten long sweet summer daysï¿½ of fever, and want of care!
And goneï¿½that day of the stormï¿½O Mother, she came to me there.
meaning:
About: Alfred Tennyson, 1st Baron Tennyson (6 August 1809 ï¿½ 6 October 1892) was Poet Laureate of the United Kingdom and is one of the most popular English poets.

Much of his verse was based on classical mythological themes, although In Memoriam was written to commemorate his best friend Arthur Hallam, a fellow poet and classmate at Trinity College, Cambridge who was engaged to Tennyson's sister but died from a cerebral hemorrhage. One of Tennyson's most famous works is Idylls of the King (1885), a series of narrative poems based entirely on King Arthur and the Arthurian tales, as thematically suggested by Sir Thomas Malory's earlier tales on the legendary king. The work was dedicated to Prince Albert, the husband of Queen Victoria. During his career, Lord Tennyson attempted drama, but his plays enjoyed little success even in his lifetime.

Tennyson wrote a number of phrases that have become commonplaces of the English language, including: "nature, red in tooth and claw", "better to have loved and lost", "Theirs not to reason why, Theirs but to do and die", and "My strength is as the strength of ten, Because my heart is pure".

He is the second most frequently quoted writer in the English language, after Shakespeare.
